svg.text: NAHUATL (NĀHUATL) / ZAPOTEC (BENE ZAZA)
UTO-AZTECAN / OTO-MANGUEAN · HISTORY & LEGACY
PLATE 47D ALT · HISTORY & CULTURAL LEGACY
— NAHUATL: HISTORY & EMPIRE —
THE AZTEC EMPIRE (1428–1521 CE)
Triple Alliance: Tenochtitlan + Texcoco + Tlacopan — dominated Central Mexico
Tenochtitlan (modern Mexico City): ~200,000-300,000 people by 1500 CE
Nahuatl became lingua franca of trade and administration across Mesoamerica
PRE-AZTEC NAHUATL: TOLTEC AND TEOTIHUACAN
Nahuatl speakers at Teotihuacan (~100 BCE–600 CE): "city of the gods"
Toltec empire (~900–1150 CE): predecessor to Aztec; Nahuatl as prestige language
Quetzalcoatl: feathered serpent deity worshipped across Mesoamerica
CONQUEST & SURVIVAL (1521 CE)
Spanish conquest 1521: Hernan Cortes, allied with Tlaxcala; Tenochtitlan fell
Nahuatl continued as administrative language under colonial rule until ~1700
Today: ~1.7M speakers in Mexico · Nahuatl dialects from Guerrero to Veracruz
"Mexicano": local name for Nahuatl in many communities; pride is resurgent
NAHUATL IN WORLD CUISINE
The Aztec market at Tlatelolco offered 200+ food varieties; Nahuatl names spread:
chocolate (xocolatl) · vanilla (tlilxochitl) · chile · tomato · avocado
SURVIVAL PHRASE
Nochi in tlacah timochihua huan tiquilnamiquia in to huiptla.
"All people we-make and we-remember toward our tomorrow."
(huiptla = the day after tomorrow; used for "future" in classical texts)
ZAPOTEC (BENE ZAZA) — HISTORY & CULTURAL LEGACY
— ZAPOTEC: THE OLDEST CIVILIZATION —
MONTE ALBAN: FIRST CITY OF THE AMERICAS (~500 BCE)
Zapotec built Monte Alban (near modern Oaxaca City) ~500 BCE
Population: ~25,000 at peak · dominated Oaxaca Valley 500 BCE–700 CE
Monuments: pyramids, ball courts, astronomical alignments, carved stelae
OLDEST WRITING SYSTEM IN THE AMERICAS
Monte Alban glyphs (~500 BCE): carved stone monuments with proto-writing
Danzantes: carved figures with glyphs, likely recording sacrificed captives
Predates Maya script by ~300 years; Zapotec claim to oldest Mesoamerican writing
MIXTEC CONQUEST & ZAPOTEC RESILIENCE
~700 CE: Monte Alban abandoned; Zapotec dispersed to regional centers
Mixtec expansion from the west — long rivalry with Zapotec city-states
1521: Spanish conquest — Zapotec lord Cosijopii negotiated rather than fought
BENITO JUAREZ: ZAPOTEC PRESIDENT OF MEXICO
Benito Juarez (1806–1872): born in Guelatao, Oaxaca, to Zapotec parents
Did not speak Spanish until age 12 · became Mexico's greatest president
"Among individuals, as among nations, respect for others' rights is peace."
ZAPOTEC TODAY
~500,000 speakers across Oaxaca · 50+ mutually unintelligible varieties
Indigenous-language instruction in some Oaxacan schools; radio stations in Zapotec
SURVIVAL PHRASE (ISTHMUS ZAPOTEC)
Bene neza cayaa dxi ti gunaa; bene neza dxita gunaa bia.
"Those who walk the road remember tomorrow; those ahead are remembered."
